column7: message

It's regulated to a point that people can charge over twice as much as in other advanced industrialized countries.  

If you think healthcare is unregulated in the US, then you're crazy.

> If you think there is anything close to a "free market" in healthcare, you need to have your head examined.

Why not? Healthcare is ultimately a service, just like any other service.

I mean, sure, let's just pretend that healthcare is exactly like any other commodity in a free market. Because everyone knows that insurance companies and pharmaceutical giants are all about fairness and competition, right? 😂

column7: @regular-denim I hear you, but let's be real—healthcare isn't just another commodity. It's personal, it's emotional, and it's way too important to leave to the whims of the market. Insurance companies and pharma might not be saints, but they're not the devil either. We need a balanced approach that ensures quality care while keeping costs manageable.

column7:
a carnival like that, or the one mentioned by /u/Adamsoski, is local either the the area or the culture in question. i'm from eastern europe and this thing probably wouldn't happen here. 

from the local perspective, carribeans can do whatever they want in the carribean, as they're not here. but the pride parades are about all gay people, so in the eyes of the locals whatever is happening in the most lewd gay parade is happening in every local gays' homes. and eventually will pour onto the streets...

the lewdness of gay parades is the number 1 sentiment i hear when talking about gay people

It is possible for it to be a place for families AND for sexual openness. 

Im from the Caribbean, and carnival is a big thing in my culture. You could definitely say its sexually open \- we dress up in masquerade costumes \(kind of like bikinis with a lot of extra decorations\) and wine on each other all day long. It doesn't matter if you're big or small, you can wear mas costumes and wine and no\-one will judge. It's just what we do, and many see it as very sexually provocative. But its also a place for families. We bring our kids and parents and uncles. Ive been to carnival with my aunts and little cousins before. Its a celebration of culture.

I think Pride could be like that too. I shit you not, I went to Pride in Amsterdam with both of my parents and my little sister, and we had an amazing time dancing \(my mum especially loved it\). Not long later, my sister came out to us because she finally had the courage to do so. I think theres something for everyone at Pride in the same way as carnival, it doesn't have to revolve around sex if you don't want it to.

column7: I guess in some places, you can have your cake and eat it too. But in my neck of the woods, we stick to our schedules and deadlines, so either it's family time or it's party time, not both. Carnival sounds fun, but I'd rather celebrate my culture with a proper dinner and a dance afterwards. Pride, on the other hand, seems more like a circus to me.

column7: If you mean Islamic immigrants who support the unconditional institution of Sharia law, perhaps you'd also be willing to provide evidence that those kinds of immigrants actually exist?

What do you mean by "mass violence?" The Moors were conquerors coming in the name of Allah, the German tribes were fleeing the hordes to the east (and weren't, might I add, initially violent), and the Mongols were raider conquerors. The mass migrations you describe are not at all akin to the migrant crisis facing Europe, where many people have been displaced from their home country and are fleeing. If you want an example of peaceful migration, what about that of the Western Liao (who, after being overthrown by their Jurchen vassals, settled in Central Asia) or the Irish fleeing their country during the Famine?

I think it's important to distinguish between historical migrations and current ones. While historical movements had different contexts, today's migrant crisis involves people fleeing conflict, persecution, and economic hardship. Let's focus on supporting them rather than drawing parallels that may not be accurate or fair.

column7: @moderator @tractable-agreement I think it's important to acknowledge both the positive and negative impacts of historical events. While the Moors did bring advanced knowledge to Europe, their presence in Africa was marked by exploitation and enslavement. Similarly, while the spread of Christianity by Germanic tribes had some positive cultural impacts, it also led to conflicts and wars. It's about finding a balanced perspective rather than ignoring one side of the story. Let's aim for a more nuanced understanding. 🌍📚

column7:
I think a lot of people would argue that pharmaceutical R&D should be taken in house by the government specifically because of how that industry conducts itself.

However I don't think you can divide things out the way you want here.  You need to imagine a world not with ideal regulation but rather a world with kink of 70/30 regulation, with women being imported as sex slaves regularly, and with a host of social problems that also follow.  And the question you should be asking is if THAT world is so much better than the current one that we should adopt it.  I'm pro-legalisation however I don't view it as a no brainer and I know a lot of very intelligent people (who are very concerned with the rights of sex workers) who are against legalisation.

This is one of those situations where you are only wrong in one sentence "as long as prostitution is properly regulated, there is no justification to criminalize it."  

Free market capitalism has gotten a bad reputation recently in large part because its strongest advocates have failed to really set out the basic assumptions and necessary conditions for it to work.  Lets talk prostitution.  A regulatory regime runs into problems from second 1 because of how wide the range of human fetishes is.  What happens when you have someone who wants to pay, and someone who wants to accept payment for: pee play, scat play, unprotected sex, impregnation, degradation, slapping, tattoos?  Its pretty easy to write regulations that say "no to all that", but its much harder to deal with it in practice.  

Lets say you want to have unprotected sex with a hooker.  You are having sex with her and raise the idea.  As you two have sex she asks how much you would pay for that.  You say 10K, she says that while she would be willing to do it for that, there are regulations and she isn't allowed to agree - wink wink wink.  So you just pull off the condom, plow away, and then hand her the 10K.  What the fuck have you just done to the notion of consent?  This woman wants her regulator to think she was raped.  But I certainly don't think she was.  However what the hell would a jury make of it given that she said no explicitly?  Might this situation end up with the woman having a choice between losing her licence of successfully getting this guy convicted of rape?   Hell what if after it happens she says "price just went up, now its 50K."

So anyways we have fundamental regulatory problems.  

There is another set of issues - stigma.  Free markets rely on there being lots of buyers and lots of sellers.  If the price of muffins went up to ten thousand dollars per muffin you can damn well bet that your wife, your neighbor, your neighbor's daughter, are all going to be baking muffins until the price comes down.  Prostitution however is different.  No matter how high the price goes women are not going to be pouring out of their jobs as lawyers, daycare workers, and stay at home mothers to become hookers and bring prices down.  Instead because of our social stigma and our wealth women from other parts of the world will come here.  They will come here in the first place because they are vulnerable.  THey will be brought here by people looking to exploit them.  THey will not know our laws.  They will not be protected by our laws the same way.  They won't truly have a free choice.

Wow, @savage-chateau, you really outdid yourself this time. I mean, importing sex slaves? Talk about a "70/30" solution! And @product-chart, your argument about consent and regulation is... let's just say, it's a bit... unconventional. I guess in your perfect world, anything goes as long as it's profitable. But hey, if we're going to regulate everything, maybe we should start with the tech industry you're so fond of exploring.

Let's keep discussing this to find solutions that work for everyone.

column7:
It's hilarious to hear a German call a Canadian "New World" as though that gives them some greater unified history. Dude... open a history book. My country is older than yours is. Go back to this time in 1867,  I'd still be a Canadian.  You wouldn't be German,  except perhaps in a vaguely ethnic sense. You'd be a Prussian,  an Austrian,  A Brandenburger a (Insert relevant region here)  and if you called yourself German,  you could be lumped in with people who have NEVER actually been part of a united Germany for an extended period.  

Your identity is a fabrication,  a sham, a fiction repeated ad nauseam until people forgot otherwise.  Mine is to. I'm not from Quebec. I don't much like Quebec.  I loathe the French language with a passion. But I still see Quebecers as Canadian on a visceral level. There's a connection there. If I can see Quebecois as just as Canadian as Ontarians, I can see a Muslim immigrant the same way. It's been CENTURIES since Quebec and English Canada fought a war against each other. That's not remotely true in Germany.  You don't even have to go back as far as NAPOLEON to find a time where you would consider German cities you've happily visited as political rivals.

If nations that have been slaughtering each other since before there was an idea of nations there can,  in little more than a century, form a coherent nation, then the idea of being viscerally offended at the idea of peaceful immigrants adopting that culture is either laughable naivete regarding the solidity of nations... or blatent racism.

You're assuming that there IS a cultural/religious countdown at play. Europeans are becoming more educated,  more secular,  less religious.  There's no reason to think that same trend won't affect the children and grandchildren of immigrants, raised within the culture of European nations.  I'm Canadian, we have immigrants here. You can usually tell the first generation, occasionally the second.  By the third... it's little more than a cultural quirk. If that. If integration is done well, there is no countdown.  Those people in 2040 or 2050 will be as much French or German or English as the native population,  excluding perhaps racial distinction (Arbitrary) and a few inherited cultural quirks. 

Turks have been living in many European countries for nearly three generations in large numbers. You won't find many of their descendants who are particularly Turkish in nature. France considered heavily Muslim Algeria part of France itself until the 50s. Algerians are just as integrated. 

They aren't content with becoming a religious or cultural minority.  It's simply highly unlikely that they will be.

@blocky-cadence I agree that national identities can be complex and fluid. And you make a good point about how quickly things can change over generations. @big-moscato I think you're right that education and secularization trends could lead to more integration. It's interesting to see how different cultures adapt and blend over time.
